text: The Supreme Court of India in Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997) laid down guidelines for the prevention of sexual harassment at the workplace. Justice J.S. Verma, delivering the judgment on 13 August 1997, invoked Article 14, Article 19, and Article 21 of the Constitution of India.

The petitioner, Ramesh Kumar Singh, filed a writ petition under Article 226 before the High Court of Delhi challenging the order dated 15 March 2022 passed by the respondent, Municipal Corporation of Delhi, terminating his employment without due process.

In accordance with Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, the complainant alleged that the respondent, Priya Sharma, issued a cheque dated 10 January 2023 for Rs. 5,00,000 which was dishonored upon presentation to the bank.

This matter is listed for hearing before Justice A.K. Patel on the next date of hearing. The counsel for both parties are directed to file their written submissions within two weeks. Registry is directed to place this matter on the supplementary list.

Relying on the precedent set in Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978), the petitioner, Delhi Medical Association, argued that the right to practice medicine falls within the ambit of Article 19(1)(g) of the Constitution and cannot be curtailed by the respondent, National Medical Commission, without following principles of natural justice.

The National Green Tribunal, presided by Justice Adarsh Kumar Goel, on 22 September 2021, directed the respondent, State of Maharashtra, to comply with the provisions of the Environment Protection Act, 1986, specifically Section 3 and Section 5, regarding industrial waste discharge into the Yamuna River.

The appellant, Sanjay Gupta, represented by Advocate R.K. Mehta, challenged the conviction under Section 302 of the Indian Penal Code before the High Court of Bombay. The trial court had convicted the appellant on 5 June 2020 based on circumstantial evidence.
Certified that this is a true copy of the original judgment. The stamp duty has been paid. The filing fee of Rs. 500 has been deposited in the registry. The case number is WP(C) 4521/2022.

The Family Court at Bangalore, presided by Judge M.S. Reddy, granted the divorce petition filed by the petitioner, Anita Desai, against the respondent, Vikram Desai, on grounds of cruelty under Section 13(1)(ia) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, on 18 November 2022.

In light of the Supreme Court ruling in K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017), the petitioner, Citizens for Digital Privacy, contended that the Aadhaar-based surveillance provisions under Section 33 of the Aadhaar Act, 2016 violate the fundamental right to privacy guaranteed under Article 21, as argued before Justice D.Y. Chandrachud.
